often think about this charlie mungerism in the ur-AI era: "you can't really buy your thinking done..."

pedantic post about citrini, citadel, keynes & AI timelines

in citadel's rebuttal to citrini's "2028 global intelligence crisis", frank flight says:

"In 1930, John Maynard Keynes wrote Economic Possibilities for our Grandchildren, predicting that productivity growth would be so powerful that by the early twenty-first century the workweek would fall to fifteen hours. He was directionally correct about productivity growth, but profoundly wrong about labor market implications"

was he though?

@tylercowen describes keynes as "the most influential policy economist", so let's look at what magnificent moustache cultivator and fellow englishman keynes actually said

the timeline discussed in keynes' essay was not as vague as "by the early twenty-first century". He said "within a hundred years" & his essay was published in 1930

by my calculation, that's by 2030

a few years is a long time in AI

links:
- Economic Possibilities for our Grandchildren (Keynes, 1930): https://t.co/UeBrdvTsoV
- GOAT: Who is the Greatest Economist of all Time, and Why Does it Matter? (Tyler Cowen, 2023): https://t.co/6VgNs6w1Mi
- THE 2028 GLOBAL INTELLIGENCE CRISIS (@Citrini7 & @alapshah1, 2026):  https://t.co/xjXLeOv7vb
- The 2026 Global Intelligence Crisis (Frank Flight, 2026): https://t.co/H8QBZZ2fsZ

no major ai news stories since breakfast
